game_name: Broken Sword: The Shadow of the Templars

danger_signals.visual_form: Khan character in disguise (clown costume, different costumes throughout)
danger_signals.meaning: immediate threat to player safety; signals encounters requiring caution

danger_signals.visual_form: aggressive NPCs pursuing the player for documents
danger_signals.meaning: danger from organized crime; signals need to evade or solve puzzles to progress

danger_signals.visual_form: dead bodies (Plantard killed by bomb, Marquet murdered in hospital)
danger_signals.meaning: world state communication that conspiracy is lethal; raises stakes for investigation

danger_signals.visual_form: sinister cult imagery and symbols related to Knights Templar conspiracy
danger_signals.meaning: systemic danger threatening multiple regions; signals deeper threat than individual criminals

safety_signals.meaning: player is not alone in investigation; cooperation reduces isolation-based danger

safety_signals.visual_form: manuscript fragments and ancient documents collected
safety_signals.meaning: knowledge acquisition provides agency and sense of forward momentum toward safety

safety_signals.visual_form: museums, cafés, hotels as neutral investigation spaces
safety_signals.meaning: temporary sanctuaries where investigation can proceed without immediate threat

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Knights Templar symbols and manuscript clues
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Khan (primary assassin in multiple disguises: clown, other costumes)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: archaeological sites (Lochmarne Ireland), historical locations across Europe and Middle East, ancient manuscripts and gems as faction artifacts

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: generic thugs pursuing protagonist for documents
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: urban locations (Paris, hotels); presence signals criminal underworld interference in treasure hunt
cafés, museums where intelligence is gathered and shared

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: assassination chain (Plantard → Marquet) revealing pattern of coordinated murders
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs become increasingly cautious; some murdered before player can interrogate them; Khan's repeated appearances across locations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: progression through European/Middle Eastern locations; artifact collection (tripod, gem, manuscript fragments)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: antagonists become more aggressive; multiple factions pursue same objects; NPCs warn protagonist of danger

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: protagonist death is possible mechanic; visual consequence of failed puzzle solutions or unsafe interactions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: game reverts to last save point; communicates that world actively threatens player agency

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: map expansion revealing new locations tied to Knights Templar clues; geographic reach from Paris to Ireland to Middle East
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs reference broader organization; assassin appears across multiple regions; hints at international coordinated threat

environmental_storytelling.example: ancient manuscript recovered from assassin's hotel safe; gem hidden at archaeological excavation site — artifacts serve as both plot devices and world-state markers
Café de la Chandelle Verte crime scene establishes Paris as central conspiracy hub; museum housing tripod reveals academic/archaeological dimension of conspiracy

environmental_storytelling.visual_manifestation: Plantard's assassination in café; Marquet's murder in hospital — deaths communicate that investigation is actively monitored and targets are eliminated

environmental_storytelling.example: Khan's multiple costumes (clown assassin) visualizes shapeshifting threat; makes player question NPC trustworthiness and environmental safety
ancient documents contain geographic and historical clues; physical artifact drives narrative progression and reveals conspiracy structure

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: appears in multiple disguises; pursues protagonist across locations; adapts strategy when thwarted; signals competence and organizational backing
shares information; works cooperatively with protagonist; has prior knowledge of assassination chain; provides stability and investigation structure
block progression until evaded or puzzle-solved; respond to player presence; indicate multiple factions seeking same treasures
some are murdered before player can fully interrogate them (Plantard, Marquet); others become cautious; communicate that investigation has lethal consequences
archaeologist Peagram has vanished; his assistant abducted; indicates conspiracy reaches into academic institutions

notes: Broken Sword uses narrative-heavy environmental storytelling typical of 1990s point-and-click adventures. Visual communication relies heavily on character presence (assassin disguises), artifact discovery, and location-based clues rather than dynamic world-state shifts. The protagonist's mortality mechanic uniquely communicates that the game world poses existential threat. Faction identity is thematic (Knights Templar conspiracy) rather than visually codified with colors/symbols; opposition is represented through antagonist appearance and criminal presence. World-state progresses through discovery chain: assassination → investigation → artifact hunt → scope expansion. The conspiracy's international reach is communicated through map expansion and location diversity.
